opp.ais_why:
• MedImpact is at Qualified with only Merlin scoped and no discovery description recorded.
• The Agentic-platform-discussed flag is not set and there are no pain points or AI-requirement captured.
• Evidence is limited to a spend + supplier interest signal, insufficient to place AI appetite above exploratory.

sources: Next_Step 2026-06-18: 'Limited scope focused on spend, no budget for this year. Executing NDA as next step to discuss how much spend'
Products: iSupplier;Merlin (scope confirmed, business case not)
metrics.narrative: No quantified business problem or ROI case has been articulated. The Next_Step entry from 2026-06-18 records 'had initial discovery. Limited scope focused on spend, no budget for this year,' but does not name the operational pain, savings target, cycle-time reduction, or cost avoidance the buyer is solving for. The scope is iSupplier + Merlin (spend analytics and AI-driven supplier intelligence), but the business case behind that scope—why now, what breaks without it, what the CFO or procurement VP would measure—is absent. The NDA is gating deeper quantification discussions ('discuss how much spend'), so the move is to use the NDA handoff to elicit the pain owner's quantified goals: annual spend under management, supplier count, manual effort, error rate, or savings opportunity. Until that surfaces, this deal has no defensible value story for the economic buyer.

sources: Task 2026-06-29: Linda Neidre (contact logged, role/title/advocacy unknown)
Next_Step 2026-06-18: 'had initial discovery' (implies Linda or another contact was engaged, but no champion behavior documented)
Contact role list: empty (no formal champion or sponsor role assigned)
champion.narrative: No champion has been identified. The only visible contact is Linda Neidre (Salesforce task 2026-06-29), whose title, role, and influence are unknown. There is no evidence of advocacy—no internal introductions, no shared buyer documents, no push to accelerate the NDA, no competitive defense. The Next_Step from 2026-06-18 records 'had initial discovery,' suggesting Linda may have been on that call, but the nature of her engagement (sponsor, influencer, or gatekeeper) is not documented. A champion must have access to the economic buyer, credibility with the decision maker, and willingness to sell Zycus internally; none of that is visible. The deal is single-threaded and 27 days old; the next 30 days must focus on multi-threading and identifying someone who will advocate for Zycus beyond the initial contact. The move is to ask Linda, during the post-NDA scope discussion, who else cares about spend visibility and AI-driven supplier insights, and to request introductions to the procurement or finance lead who controls the 2027 budget.

competition.narrative: No competitor has been identified. The Salesforce Competitor field is empty, and no competitive mention appears in Next_Step, tasks, or any available Salesforce text field. The Next_Step from 2026-06-18 states 'no budget for this year,' positioning this as a 2027 decision, but does not indicate whether MedImpact is evaluating alternatives, running an RFP, or sole-sourcing. For a healthcare payer procurement deal, typical competitors include Coupa, SAP Ariba, GEP, Ivalua, and Jaggaer; it is plausible this is early and competitive positioning has not yet emerged, or that the buyer is exploring internally first. The risk is assuming sole-source when a competitive process is planned. The move is to use the post-NDA scope discussion to ask directly: 'Are you evaluating other vendors, or is this a sole-source discussion?' If competitive, map the shortlist and the wedge (Merlin AI negotiation agent on supplier price history and contract intelligence is the differentiator). If sole-sourced, confirm why and secure that positioning in writing.

sources: Next_Step 2026-06-18: 'Limited scope focused on spend, no budget for this year' (scope stated, pain not)
Products: iSupplier;Merlin (tools implied by need, need not articulated)
identify_pain.narrative: No specific operational or financial pain has been captured. The scope is spend analytics (iSupplier) and AI-driven supplier intelligence (Merlin), which implies pain around spend visibility, supplier risk, or procurement efficiency, but the buyer has not articulated what is broken today. The Next_Step from 2026-06-18 notes 'limited scope focused on spend' and 'no budget for this year,' but does not name the pain owner (e.g., CPO, VP Procurement, CFO), the trigger (e.g., audit finding, supplier failure, cost-reduction mandate), or the consequence of inaction. For a healthcare payer managing supplier spend, common pains include lack of real-time spend visibility, supplier concentration risk, formulary cost leakage, and manual rebate reconciliation—but none of these has been confirmed. The move is to use the NDA discussion to ask: 'What's driving the need for better spend visibility now? What breaks if you wait another year?' and to identify the executive owner of that pain.

sources: Next_Step 2026-06-18: 'Executing NDA as next step to discuss how much spend and if we can expand into additional modules' (NDA in flight, rest of process not documented)
paper_process.narrative: NDA is in flight (Next_Step 2026-06-18: 'Executing NDA as next step'), which is the first contracting artifact and gates deeper scope and pricing discussions. This is a positive signal—legal engagement has started—but the rest of the paper process is unknown: whether MedImpact uses a standard MSA, whether InfoSec or compliance review is required (likely, given healthcare payer regulatory environment), whether procurement runs a formal sourcing process, and whether an SI or implementation partner is involved. The close date is June 2027, giving ~11 months for legal, procurement, and contracting, which is feasible for an enterprise deal if the process is structured. The move is to confirm, during the post-NDA scope discussion, the full contracting path: MSA, SOW, security review, and any procurement or compliance gates.

sources: Contact role list: none on record
Next_Step 2026-06-18: 'no budget for this year' (implies 2027 budget cycle, but no budget owner named)
Task 2026-06-29: Linda Neidre (role/title unknown, no evidence of budget authority)
economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has been identified or engaged. The only visible contact is Linda Neidre (Salesforce task 2026-06-29), whose title, role, and budget authority are unknown. The Next_Step from 2026-06-18 states 'no budget for this year,' positioning this as a 2027 budget cycle deal, but does not name who controls that 2027 procurement budget—no CFO, CPO, or VP Procurement is mentioned. For a $120K SaaS + Merlin deal at an enterprise healthcare payer, the economic buyer is typically a VP or C-level procurement/finance leader; none has surfaced. The NDA discussion and scope expansion conversation must be used to map the budget owner and secure a direct meeting. Until an EB is named and engaged, the forecast is speculative.

sources: Next_Step 2026-06-18: 'no budget for this year. Executing NDA as next step' (one gate named, broader process not)
Task 2026-06-29: Linda Neidre (single contact, no committee visible)
decision_process.narrative: The decision process is unknown. The deal is 27 days old, in Qualified stage, with one known contact (Linda Neidre, 2026-06-29 task) and no documented buying committee, approval chain, or timeline beyond the June 2027 close date. The Next_Step from 2026-06-18 states 'no budget for this year,' positioning this as a 2027 budget cycle decision, but does not describe the steps between now and then—who else must be involved, whether procurement or InfoSec will run a formal review, whether an RFP or pilot is planned, or how many vendors will be evaluated. No mention of legal, finance approval, or executive sponsorship. The NDA is the current gate; once executed, the process must be mapped: ask Linda (or whoever leads the NDA discussion) to walk through the buying steps, stakeholders, and gates from scope confirmation to contract signature.

sources: Next_Step 2026-06-18: 'Limited scope focused on spend… discuss how much spend and if we can expand into additional modules' (scope stated, criteria not)
Products: iSupplier;Merlin (what, but not how they will decide)
decision_criteria.narrative: No formal decision criteria have been captured. The scope is documented (iSupplier for spend analytics, Merlin for AI-driven supplier insights), and the Next_Step from 2026-06-18 notes potential expansion into 'additional modules,' but the evaluation framework—what makes a solution acceptable, how vendors will be scored, what use cases or test scenarios will gate shortlisting—is absent. There is no RFI, RFP, or BRD reference, and no indication that MedImpact has shared a requirements list, scorecard, or proof-of-concept plan. The deal is 27 days old and still in Qualified; criteria typically emerge during Formal Evaluation or after the NDA when deeper scoping begins. The move is to use the post-NDA scope discussion to ask for the buyer's evaluation plan and decision rubric, and to confirm whether this is sole-sourced or competitive.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Merlin (AI supplier intelligence) is included in the product scope, indicating AI interest. No AIS_Score, AIS_Status, or AIS_Why field data, and no Avoma calls to assess depth of AI appetite. The buyer's AI maturity and expectations are unknown. The post-NDA discussion should probe: are they exploring AI-driven procurement, or is Merlin a nice-to-have add-on? Position Merlin as the strategic differentiator (AI negotiation agent, supplier risk prediction, contract intelligence) rather than a feature.

items.expected_effect: Expands stakeholder map, identifies Economic Buyer and potential Champion, reduces single-thread risk. NDA handoff is a natural moment to ask 'who else should be in the room?'

items.action: During the post-NDA scope discussion, elicit the quantified business problem: ask the buyer to describe the pain driving the need for better spend visibility (annual spend under management, supplier count, manual effort, error rate, savings opportunity), the executive owner of that pain, and the consequence of waiting another year. items.expected_effect: Surfaces the business case and pain owner (likely CPO or VP Procurement), anchors the AI value prop, and creates a quantified ROI narrative for the Economic Buyer. Without this, deal has no defensible value story.

items.action: Confirm the competitive landscape: ask directly whether MedImpact is evaluating other vendors (Coupa, Ariba, GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer) or if this is a sole-source discussion. If competitive, map the shortlist and the buyer's decision rubric. If sole-sourced, confirm why and secure that positioning in writing (e.g., 'we are exploring AI-driven procurement with Zycus exclusively').

items.expected_effect: Clarifies whether this is a sole-source or multi-vendor evaluation, enables proactive competitive positioning (Merlin as the AI wedge), and avoids being blindsided by a competitor later.

items.action: Map the decision process and approval chain: ask the buyer to walk through the steps from scope confirmation to contract signature (who else must approve, whether procurement or InfoSec will run a formal review, whether an RFP or pilot is planned, what gates exist between now and June 2027 close). items.action: Identify and develop a champion: during the post-NDA multi-threading, look for a buyer contact who has credibility with the Economic Buyer, urgency around the pain, and willingness to advocate for Zycus internally. Share a win story (healthcare payer case study with Merlin ROI) and ask them to sell it internally. If no champion emerges, escalate to the deal owner's manager for an executive sponsor play.
